ctx.name: Riley
ctx.notes: Riley is in week two and is moving quickly through codebase orientation, but he has mostly learned from docs and async notes rather than direct team contact. I want to check whether he feels clear on ownership boundaries, incident expectations, and where to ask for context before making architecture assumptions. There is a risk he looks productive while building an internal map that differs from how the team actually operates. This check-in should identify missing context early and convert it into concrete onboarding support.

focusPoints.label: How he’s landing with the team day-to-day.
focusPoints.reason: Riley is in week two and has mostly learned from docs and async notes rather than direct team contact. One possibility is he’s moving fast but missing tacit norms and how decisions really get made, so it’s worth clarifying what direct interactions he’s had so far.

focusPoints.label: Ownership boundaries and what “good” means.
focusPoints.reason: Riley’s onboarding focus should include clear ownership boundaries so he doesn’t build an internal map that differs from how the team actually operates. For a Mid backend engineer, role clarity prevents wasted effort and reduces the chance of late rework when expectations surface.

focusPoints.label: Where he should ask for context early.
focusPoints.reason: Riley could be at risk of making architecture assumptions without the right context. Clarifying which questions he should ask, and when, can turn missing context into concrete onboarding support before it compounds.

focusPoints.label: Incident expectations and escalation norms.
focusPoints.reason: Riley’s manager specifically wants to check incident expectations. At Mid level, getting escalation and expectations right early is what keeps him productive while still protecting the team during incidents.

transcript.question: What has been most on your mind in your first two weeks here, Riley?
transcript.answer: I am comfortable reading the services, but I am less sure how decisions are really made here.

transcript.question: When you say 'how decisions are really made' — what part is least clear?
transcript.answer: I can trace most request paths now, though I still miss historical context behind certain tradeoffs.

transcript.question: trace most request — can you say more about what that means for you right now?
transcript.answer: I have not been fully clear on who is on point for each domain when priorities conflict.

transcript.question: What feels clearest to you about what you own right now?
transcript.answer: I am trying not to interrupt people, so sometimes I keep questions in a notes doc instead of asking.

transcript.question: What kinds of questions are sitting in that notes doc right now?
transcript.answer: I would benefit from a clearer guide for what to escalate quickly versus what to batch.

transcript.question: What would a useful escalate-versus-batch guide need to cover?
transcript.answer: The runbook helped, but some practical handoff details only show up during live incidents.

transcript.question: What context are you still waiting on before you change a design or make an architecture call?
transcript.answer: A short weekly architecture review with someone senior would help me calibrate faster.

transcript.question: What would make the next two weeks feel steadier for you, Riley?
transcript.answer: If I know exactly what ownership I should take this month, I can contribute with more confidence.

transcript.question: If you had to cut your work in half tomorrow, what would you drop first — and what's non-negotiable?
transcript.answer: I will ask one architecture question in the next incident review instead of keeping it in my notes doc.
